claim_text: MRI embeddings predict MCI to AD conversion
head: linear probe

site_scanner.detail: single scanner/site — no acquisition confound to test

brain_age.detail: brain-age R2=0.39, MAE=4.9yr; effect retained 72% after brain-age-gap control

biomarker_anchor.detail: no plasma p-tau217 / GFAP coverage at usable n — cannot anchor (route to ADNI/EPAD)

replication.detail: single site/cohort — no held-out split available

caveats: Biomarker anchor unavailable (no plasma p-tau217/GFAP coverage): claim cannot be promoted until molecularly anchored.
Completeness: 3/5 gauntlet tests could not run; score is renormalized over the tests that did.
'Partially robust' is not 'robust' — survives some but not all challenges.
Verdict is provisional and conditional on the scanner/site distribution of any replication cohort.
Brain-age adjustment uses a proxy control; residual aging signal cannot be excluded.
Any biomarker anchor is complete-case only and may not generalize.
Leakage is bounded, not eliminated — see the published batch-effect audits (‘Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,’ arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)).
‘Partially robust’ is not ‘robust’. The score renormalizes over the tests that ran, so a middling verdict can hide a dimension that quietly failed — read the per-test results, not just the headline.

The brain-age control is a proxy, not a gold standard: it is embedding-derived (this cohort's fit: R2=0.39, MAE=4.9yr), so residual generic-aging signal can survive the adjustment and masquerade as disease-specific.
There is no usable plasma p-tau217 / GFAP anchor here, so the molecular gate is unmet — the finding rests on imaging alone.
N is small (~86); at this size AUCs are noisy and a single site's idiosyncrasy can dominate — treat effect sizes as wide-interval estimates.

The leakage test audits the embeddings with the same linear-probe family it uses for the outcome, so it bounds — but cannot fully rule out — shared confounding; this is the mechanic quantified in ‘Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking’ (arXiv:2606.09189 (2026)), which we cite rather than claim.

evidence_ledger.detail: single scanner/site — no acquisition confound to test
brain-age R2=0.39, MAE=4.9yr; effect retained 72% after brain-age-gap control
no plasma p-tau217 / GFAP coverage at usable n — cannot anchor (route to ADNI/EPAD)
single site/cohort — no held-out split available

narration: The claim “MRI embeddings predict MCI to AD conversion” lands at “partially robust” (62/100). The naive effect (AUC 0.52 separating MCI converters from MCI non-converters) is real on paper, but the referee's job is to ask what else could produce it. It sits below the promotion floor, so the honest read is that acquisition, demographics or generic aging can plausibly account for it, and it should not consume a scientist's quarter without stronger evidence. Where it stops being true: that a molecular anchor exists at all — with no p-tau217 / GFAP correlation measured here, the finding rests on imaging alone and would not survive a demand for orthogonal pathology. That the same frozen embeddings can predict scanner/site as well as outcome is documented prior art (“Batch Effects in Brain Foundation Model Embeddings,” arXiv:2604.14441 (2026)); NeuroAD Referee only runs the audit and issues the verdict.
